title: Romans
author: Paul, the apostle, dictated to his secretary Tertius (Romans 16:22)
date: ~AD 56–57, written from Corinth during Paul's third missionary journey
setting: Paul had not yet visited Rome but planned to do so on his way to Spain (Romans 15:24). He writes to prepare the Roman church — largely Gentile but with a significant Jewish-Christian presence — for his visit and to set out his gospel comprehensively.
purpose: The most systematic exposition of the gospel in all of Paul's letters. Romans traces the human problem (universal condemnation), the divine solution (justification by faith), the life of the justified (sanctification), the place of Israel in God's purposes, and the practical shape of Christian community.
themes: Christian ethics and life in community

outline.label: Introduction and Theme: The Righteousness of God
Justification by Faith: The Righteousness Revealed
Sanctification: Dead to Sin, Alive to God
Israel and the Gospel: God's Faithfulness
Christian Ethics: Living as Justified People

before.label: Genesis 15 — Abraham credited as righteous through faith
Paul's third missionary journey; Corinthian correspondence
1–2 Corinthians — Paul addresses the church's problems

after.label: Paul journeys to Jerusalem with the Gentile collection
Paul arrested in Jerusalem; two years of Roman custody begin
Prison letters: Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon

key_people.name: Paul
key_people.role: Author and apostle; writing to a church he did not found, Romans is his most systematic exposition of the gospel — intended both to introduce himself theologically and to address the Jewish-Gentile tensions in the Roman churches

key_people.name: Abraham
key_people.role: The central OT figure in Paul's argument (ch. 4); his faith-based justification before circumcision establishes that the principle of justification by faith is older than and independent of the Mosaic covenant

key_people.name: Adam
key_people.role: The figure whose one trespass brought condemnation to all (5:12-21); his role as representative head is the structural parallel to Christ as the second Adam whose obedience brings justification to many

context: Romans was written c. AD 57 from Corinth, toward the end of Paul's third missionary journey, as he planned a trip to Rome and then Spain. The Roman church was a complex mixture of Jewish and Gentile Christians, and the tensions between these groups provide the pastoral context for the letter's theology. Romans is Paul's most sustained and systematic exposition of the gospel, written not to resolve a specific crisis but to lay a theological foundation for a church Paul hopes to partner with.
christ_connection: Romans presents the most comprehensive NT account of what Christ's death and resurrection accomplish. His death is a propitiation (3:25) that satisfies God's justice, a justification of the ungodly (4:5), a reconciliation of enemies (5:10), a condemnation of sin in the flesh (8:3), and a payment of the debt the law demanded (8:1-4). His resurrection is the guarantee of our own (6:5; 8:11) and the foundation of our hope (8:34). Romans 5's parallel between Adam and Christ argues that what Christ undoes is precisely what Adam did — so the scope of grace is at least as wide as the scope of sin.

key_verses.note: Paul's thesis: 'the gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes... For in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith for faith' — justification by faith as the gospel's core
'For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and are justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus' — the gospel in its most compressed form
'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' — the conclusion of the argument of chs. 1-7 and the foundation of the assurance of chs. 8-11
'Present your bodies as a living sacrifice... be transformed by the renewal of your mind' — the hinge between the gospel (1-11) and its ethical implications (12-16)

themes_detail.title: Justification by Faith Alone
themes_detail.text: Romans 1-5 is Paul's foundational argument for justification by faith: all humans (both Gentile and Jew) are under God's condemnation; God's righteousness is revealed in the gospel as a gift received through faith; Abraham is the paradigmatic example that this principle precedes and is independent of the Mosaic law. The term 'justified' (dikaioo) is forensic — a declaration of right standing before God — not a description of moral improvement. Its ground is Christ's obedience and atoning death; its instrument is faith alone; it is entirely by grace. This is Paul's answer to the fundamental question: how can sinful humans stand before a holy God?

themes_detail.title: The New Life in the Spirit
themes_detail.text: Romans 6-8 addresses the question that justification by grace alone inevitably raises: does this encourage sin? Paul's answer is a sustained argument about what union with Christ means: those united to Christ in his death are united to him in his resurrection, so that sin is no longer their master (6:1-14). The law, far from producing righteousness, actually stirs up sin (7:7-25) — a function the Spirit now fulfils positively (8:1-17). Chapter 8 is the book's experiential and pastoral climax: in the Spirit, Christians are sons, co-heirs with Christ, and recipients of a hope that encompasses the entire creation's renewal (8:18-25).

themes_detail.title: Israel, the Gentiles, and the Mystery of God's Plan
themes_detail.text: Romans 9-11, often neglected in systematic treatments of the book, addresses the most pressing theological question of Paul's day: if the gospel is for all nations, has God's word to Israel failed (9:6)? Paul's answer unfolds in three stages: God's election has always been selective within Israel (9:6-29); Israel's stumbling is partly responsible for the Gentile mission (10:19-11:12); and Israel's final restoration will accompany the fullness of the Gentile ingathering (11:25-32). The doxology closing chapter 11 (11:33-36) is Paul's acknowledgment that this mystery exceeds his comprehension — but not God's sovereignty.

themes_detail.title: The Gospel's Ethical Implications
themes_detail.text: Romans 12-15 does not attach ethics to the gospel as an afterthought but as its direct consequence: 'I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God' (12:1). The mercy of the gospel is the motivation and foundation for the transformed life. The ethical section covers community relationships (12:3-16), response to enemies (12:17-21), submission to governing authorities (13:1-7), love as the law's fulfilment (13:8-10), and the strong-weak tensions in the Roman churches (14:1-15:13). The model throughout is Christ himself: 'Welcome one another as Christ has welcomed you' (15:7).
